title: Around Gauss Circle Problem: Hardy's Conjecture and the Distribution of Lattice Points Near Circles
speaker: Igor Wigman
abstract: This talk is based on a joint work with Steve Lester. We review the Gauss circle problem, and Hardy's conjecture regarding the order of magnitude of the remainder term. It is attempted to rigorously formulate the folklore heuristics behind Hardy's conjecture. Some weaker forms of the likely statement are proved to support it.

proof_stages.label: Classical Gauss-circle error and Hardy exponent
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the lattice-point count in a disk, Gauss’s elementary area comparison giving an O(R) error, the known upper and lower bounds for the centered error, and Hardy’s conjectural exponent 1/2.

proof_stages.label: Boundary-cell heuristic and annular area variables
proof_stages.summary: The expected square-root-size fluctuation is motivated by treating boundary cells as random Bernoulli contributions, after which the annular set Gamma_R and the cell-area quantities A_R(lambda) are defined and connected back to the Gauss error.

proof_stages.label: One-point distribution theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main one-point results state that the mean of A_R(lambda) is asymptotically 1/2, its variance tends to an explicit positive constant c_0, and the empirical distribution over Gamma_R converges to a limiting random variable A_infinity.

proof_stages.label: Angular correlations and independence conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Ordering the annular lattice points by angle leads to fixed-lag correlations C_k, the conjecture C_k to 0 as k grows, and supporting results including numerics, Cesaro-type vanishing on average, and angular-window variants.

proof_stages.label: Shifted polar equidistribution and local half-plane model
proof_stages.summary: Using shifted polar coordinates lambda=(R+r_lambda)e^{i theta_lambda}, the pairs (r_lambda,theta_lambda) are shown to equidistribute, and the circle is locally linearized so that A_R(lambda) is modeled by the area of a rotated and shifted unit square on one side of a line, yielding A_infinity and the variance computation.

proof_stages.label: Refined pair limit with common angle
proof_stages.summary: The naive expectation that distant angular neighbors converge to independent copies of A_infinity is corrected: after taking R to infinity first, the limiting pair shares the same tangent angle but has independent radial shifts, and this averaged pair limit implies the averaged correlation theorem.

proof_stages.label: Correlation model in a rotated strip
proof_stages.summary: For fixed lag k, the local sector near lambda_j is replaced by a translated rotated semi-infinite rectangle, the kth subsequent lattice point is modeled by ordering projections along the long side, C_k is identified with a covariance in this model, and the proof reduces average vanishing to projection equidistribution while controlling arc-versus-rectangle and ordering errors.

examples.name: Flat-torus bouncing-ball geodesics
examples.note: An audience comment relates Hardy-type lower bounds to a flat-torus semiclassical picture with one-parameter families of equal-length geodesics, illustrating that the lower-bound phenomenon has a spectral-geometric analogue rather than producing the speaker's proof idea.
examples.engagement_reason: The torus bouncing-ball geodesics are invoked as a geometric explanation for a lower bound, but no calculation or construction is carried out on them.

passes.reason: A same-length parallel closed-geodesic family on a flat torus is a collapsed translation-symmetric feature used as motivation rather than as a refutation.
They are a non-isolated one-parameter family of parallel closed geodesics on a highly regular flat torus, cited as geometric motivation for the lower bound.
Flat-torus bouncing-ball geodesics form highly regular non-isolated equal-length families, used as the geometric obstruction to a too-small remainder term.
Flat torus geodesics form a highly regular non-isolated one-parameter family of equal-length periodic orbits used to explain a lower bound, not to refute a claim.

examples.note: The circle is compared with ellipses or general convex curves to emphasize the locality of the boundary-cell analysis and possible generalizations, but the talk’s results are formulated for the circle.
examples.engagement_reason: Ellipses and general convex curves are discussed as possible replacements for the circle, with comments that the analysis would be local/different, but no specific curve is worked through.

examples.name: Higher-dimensional lattice-point problems
examples.note: A question about dimensions three and four illustrates how the same annular philosophy may or may not extend, with dimension four degenerating because sums of four squares create large jumps.
examples.engagement_reason: Higher-dimensional analogues are only discussed in general terms, including stated facts about dimensions three and four, without applying the method concretely.

passes.reason: Higher-dimensional lattice-point analogues are systematic variants raised as a scope question rather than a one-off or refutation.

examples.name: Horizontal unrotated rectangle
examples.note: The rational-direction strip, where all relevant lattice-point projections lie on the short-side origin and give a delta measure, is used as a counterexample showing why projection equidistribution must be restricted to generic angles.
examples.engagement_reason: The unrotated horizontal rectangle is used as a concrete exceptional case, with the lattice points identified as lying on the x-axis and the projection distribution concluded to be a delta measure.

passes.reason: The axis-aligned rational orientation collapses the short-side projections to a delta mass and is used to show equidistribution fails for every angle.
The axis-aligned unrotated rectangle makes all short-side projections collapse to a delta mass, disproving equidistribution for every angle.

examples.name: Irrational triangle and straight-line discrepancy
examples.note: The Q&A compares the circular problem with counting under an irrational triangular or straight-line boundary, illustrating that lower bounds become a problem about fractional parts and that curvature matters.
examples.engagement_reason: The irrational triangle/straight-line discrepancy case is cited in the Q&A with known reductions and lower-bound facts, but no particular triangle or discrepancy computation is performed.

passes.reason: The zero-curvature straight-side triangle case is a flattened boundary case used to show curved-circle heuristics do not automatically extend.
The irrational triangle reduces the curved-boundary problem to flat straight-line discrepancy, showing the circle-style square-root heuristic needs curvature.
The irrational straight-edge/triangle case is a zero-curvature flattened boundary used to show curved-boundary square-root heuristics need not apply.
The irrational triangle or straight-line case has flat zero-curvature boundary behavior and is used as an obstruction to curvature-free versions of the circle heuristics.
The irrational triangle/straight-line case has flat zero-curvature boundary and is invoked to show circle-style square-root lower-bound behavior does not extend to flat boundaries.

examples.name: Alternative planar tilings instead of unit squares
examples.note: A final question asks about replacing the square cells by other planar tiles, illustrating that the limiting distributions would depend on the chosen local tile shape though the same general strategy should persist.
